name: domain-fusion-economics
description: Use web_search to search for 'fusion energy economics cost per MWh competitiveness solar wind comparison'. Results will overlap with earlier fusion searches. Can fusion compete economically with renewables? What is the projected cost of fusion electricity? Keep response under 100 words.

ground_truth.reference_answer: Fusion energy faces a steep cost challenge against renewables. Solar PV is projected at $25-30/MWh and onshore wind at ~$35/MWh by 2050, while early fusion plants are estimated at $150+/MWh, needing to fall to $50-100/MWh for commercial viability. CFS CEO Bob Mumgaard stated that $100/MWh lets fusion enter the market, while $50/MWh lets it win. However, fusion offers a critical advantage as firm, carbon-free baseload power that operates 24/7 regardless of weather, complementing intermittent renewables. When storage and grid balancing costs are included, fusion becomes more competitive. An MIT study found that fusion availability could reduce global decarbonization costs by $3.6 trillion, underscoring its systemic economic value beyond raw LCOE comparisons.
key_facts: Early fusion plants are estimated to cost $150+/MWh, needing to fall to $50-100/MWh for competitiveness
Solar PV is projected at $25-30/MWh and onshore wind at ~$35/MWh by 2050
CFS CEO stated $100/MWh enters the market, $50/MWh wins the market
Fusion provides firm, carbon-free baseload power unlike intermittent renewables
MIT study found fusion availability could reduce global decarbonization costs by $3.6 trillion
